quiz_questions.question_text: What is a free-body diagram?

options.text: A diagram showing all forces acting on a single object
A picture showing what an object looks like in detail
A graph showing how speed changes over some time
A chart comparing different objects by their masses

quiz_questions.explanation: A free-body diagram is a simplified drawing that shows all the forces acting on a single object. The object is usually represented as a dot or simple shape, and arrows represent each force.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a free-body diagram, what do the arrows represent?

options.text: The direction the object is moving
The forces acting on the object
The speed of the object at each point
The mass of the object in kilograms

quiz_questions.explanation: In a free-body diagram, each arrow represents a force. The direction of the arrow shows which way the force pushes or pulls, and the length of the arrow represents the magnitude (size) of the force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A book sits on a table. How many forces would typically appear in its free-body diagram?

options.text: Zero forces acting on it
Only one force acting on it
Two forces: gravity and normal force
Four forces in all four directions

quiz_questions.explanation: A book on a table has two forces: gravity pulling it downward and the normal force from the table pushing it upward. These two forces are equal and opposite, keeping the book stationary.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which direction does the force of gravity always point in a free-body diagram?

options.text: To the left of the object
Upward away from Earth
To the right of the object
Downward toward Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: The force of gravity always points straight downward toward the center of the Earth. In a free-body diagram, the gravity arrow always points down regardless of the object's orientation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the length of an arrow in a free-body diagram represent?

options.text: The magnitude (size) of the force
The duration the force is applied
The mass of the object involved
The temperature of the object

quiz_questions.explanation: The length of an arrow in a free-body diagram is proportional to the magnitude of the force. A longer arrow means a stronger force, and a shorter arrow means a weaker force.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a free-body diagram for an object being pushed across a floor, which force opposes the motion?

options.text: The force of gravity on the object
The normal force from the floor surface
The friction force from the floor
The applied pushing force on it

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction is the force that opposes the motion of an object sliding across a surface. In the free-body diagram, friction points in the opposite direction to the motion or applied force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal force in a free-body diagram?

options.text: The force pulling the object toward Earth
The support force perpendicular to a surface
The force that makes an object move faster
The friction that slows the object down

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the support force exerted by a surface on an object resting on it. It is always perpendicular (at a right angle) to the surface and pushes the object away from the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: Should a free-body diagram include forces that the object exerts on other objects?

options.text: Yes, all forces involving the object should be shown
Yes, but only the largest forces it exerts on others
No, only forces acting ON the object are included
No, free-body diagrams never include any forces

quiz_questions.explanation: A free-body diagram only shows forces acting ON the object, not forces the object exerts on other things. This is important because we are analyzing the motion of that single object.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is the object typically represented in a free-body diagram?

options.text: As a detailed realistic drawing of the object
As a dot or simple box shape in the center
As a photograph of the object being studied
The object is not shown in the diagram at all

quiz_questions.explanation: In a free-body diagram, the object is simplified to a dot or simple shape like a box. The focus is on the forces, not on what the object looks like, so details are not needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: If an object is accelerating to the right, what can you conclude about the forces in its free-body diagram?

options.text: All forces point to the right only
The net force points to the right
All forces are perfectly balanced
There are no forces on the object

quiz_questions.explanation: If an object accelerates to the right, the net force must point to the right (Newton's second law). There can be forces in other directions too, but the total of all forces combined points right.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a free-body diagram used for?

options.text: To visually identify and analyze all forces on an object
To measure the exact temperature of an object precisely
To determine the chemical composition of an object
To calculate the volume and density of any object

quiz_questions.explanation: Free-body diagrams are used to visually identify and analyze all forces acting on an object. They help physicists and students determine the net force and predict how the object will move.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'normal force' mean?

options.text: The gravitational pull toward the center of Earth
The force a surface exerts perpendicular to itself
The sideways friction between two surfaces
The force applied by a person pushing an object

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the perpendicular contact force exerted by a surface on an object. 'Normal' in physics means perpendicular (at 90 degrees). A table pushes up on a book sitting on it with a normal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'applied force' mean in a free-body diagram?

options.text: A push or pull exerted on the object by something
The force of gravity pulling the object downward
The resistance from friction on the ground
The upward push from the surface below it

quiz_questions.explanation: An applied force is a push or pull directly exerted on an object by a person, machine, or another object. It is shown as an arrow in the direction the push or pull acts.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'tension force' mean?

options.text: The pulling force transmitted through a rope or string
The push force exerted by a compressed spring coil
The force of gravity acting on an object near Earth
The friction force between two surfaces in contact

quiz_questions.explanation: Tension is the pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, cable, or chain when it is pulled tight. In a free-body diagram, tension points along the rope away from the object.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'net force' mean?

options.text: Only the largest single force on the object
Only the smallest single force on the object
The combined effect of all forces on the object
The force of gravity by itself on the object

quiz_questions.explanation: The net force is the vector sum of all forces acting on an object. It determines the object's acceleration. If the net force is zero, the object stays at rest or moves at constant velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'weight' mean in a free-body diagram?

options.text: The same thing as mass measured in kilograms
The force of gravity on the object, pointing down
The upward force from the surface below it
The friction that opposes the object's movement

quiz_questions.explanation: In a free-body diagram, weight is the force of gravity acting on the object, always pointing downward. Weight equals mass times gravitational acceleration (W = mg) and is measured in newtons.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'friction force' mean in a free-body diagram?

options.text: A force that opposes sliding between two surfaces
A force that pulls objects toward the center of Earth
A force that pushes objects upward off the surface
A force that makes objects heavier than they are

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction is a contact force that opposes the sliding motion between two surfaces. In a free-body diagram, friction always points opposite to the direction of motion or the direction the object would tend to slide.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'equilibrium' mean in a free-body diagram?

options.text: The object is accelerating in one direction quickly
All forces are balanced and the net force is zero
The object has no forces acting on it at all
The object is moving at its maximum possible speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Equilibrium means the net force on the object is zero because all forces are balanced. The object is either at rest or moving at constant velocity. The forces cancel out in all directions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'contact force' mean?

options.text: A force that acts without objects touching each other
A force that requires physical touching between objects
A force that only exists in outer space vacuum
A force that can never be shown on any diagram

quiz_questions.explanation: A contact force requires physical contact between objects. Examples include friction, normal force, tension, and applied force. Gravity is a non-contact force because it acts at a distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'non-contact force' mean?

options.text: A force that only works when objects are touching
A force that acts even without physical contact
A force that cannot exist in the real world
A force that is too weak to be ever measured

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-contact force acts on an object without physical contact. Gravity is the most common example: Earth pulls a ball downward without touching it. Magnetic and electric forces are also non-contact.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student pushes a box to the right across a rough floor at constant velocity. How many forces appear in the free-body diagram?

options.text: Two forces: gravity and the normal force only
Four forces: gravity, normal, applied, and friction
One force: only the applied pushing force here
Three forces: gravity, normal, and applied force

quiz_questions.explanation: Four forces act on the box: gravity (down), normal force (up), applied force (right, from the push), and friction (left, opposing motion). Since velocity is constant, all four forces are balanced.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lamp hangs from a ceiling by a cord. What forces appear in its free-body diagram?

options.text: Gravity pulling down and tension pulling up
Only gravity pulling the lamp downward here
Normal force pushing up and friction to the side
Applied force pushing up and drag to the side

quiz_questions.explanation: The lamp has gravity pulling it downward and tension in the cord pulling it upward. Since the lamp hangs still, these forces are equal and opposite. There is no normal force because the lamp does not rest on a surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child pulls a wagon to the right with a rope. The wagon moves at constant speed. In the free-body diagram, what direction does friction point?

options.text: To the right, same direction as the pull
Straight downward toward the ground below
Straight upward away from the ground here
To the left, opposing the direction of motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction always opposes the direction of motion. Since the wagon moves to the right, friction points to the left. At constant speed, the tension pulling right equals the friction pulling left.

quiz_questions.question_text: A skydiver falls at terminal velocity. In the free-body diagram, how do the force arrows compare?

options.text: The gravity arrow is much longer than the drag arrow
The drag arrow is much longer than the gravity arrow
The gravity and drag arrows are equal in length
There are no force arrows at terminal velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: At terminal velocity, gravity (down) and air resistance (up) are balanced. In the free-body diagram, both arrows are the same length because the forces are equal in magnitude.

quiz_questions.question_text: A soccer ball is in the air after being kicked (ignore air resistance). What does its free-body diagram show?

options.text: Only one force: gravity pulling it downward
Two forces: gravity down and a kick force forward
Three forces: gravity, normal force, and friction
No forces at all while it is in the air

quiz_questions.explanation: Once the ball is in the air and air resistance is ignored, the only force acting on it is gravity. The kick force was a contact force that stopped when the foot lost contact. Normal force and friction require surface contact.

quiz_questions.question_text: A book is placed on a tilted ramp. Which forces appear in its free-body diagram?

options.text: Only gravity pulling it straight downward
Gravity, normal force perpendicular to ramp, and friction along ramp
Only normal force and friction from the ramp surface
Gravity and an applied force pushing it up the ramp

quiz_questions.explanation: On a ramp, the book has gravity (straight down), a normal force (perpendicular to the ramp surface), and friction (along the ramp surface, opposing the tendency to slide down). The normal force is not straight up on a ramp.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person stands still on the ground. In their free-body diagram, the normal force arrow and gravity arrow are equal length. What does this tell you?

options.text: The person is accelerating upward away from ground
The person is sinking into the ground slowly
The forces are balanced and the person is in equilibrium
The person is about to start moving to the right

quiz_questions.explanation: Equal-length arrows pointing in opposite directions mean the forces are balanced (net force = 0). The person is in equilibrium, which is why they remain stationary. No acceleration occurs.

quiz_questions.question_text: A dog pulls a sled with a 50 N force to the right, but friction provides 30 N to the left. What is the net horizontal force on the sled?

options.text: 80 N to the right direction
30 N to the left direction
20 N to the right direction
50 N to the left direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Net horizontal force = 50 N right - 30 N left = 20 N to the right. The pulling force is greater than friction, so the sled accelerates to the right with a 20 N net force.

quiz_questions.question_text: An elevator accelerates upward with a person inside. In the person's free-body diagram, which force is larger?

options.text: Gravity is larger than the normal force here
The normal force is larger than gravity here
Both forces are exactly equal in this situation
There is no gravity inside an elevator at all

quiz_questions.explanation: For the person to accelerate upward, the net force must point upward. This means the normal force (up) must be greater than gravity (down). This is why you feel heavier when an elevator starts going up.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball is thrown horizontally and is in mid-air. A student draws a free-body diagram with gravity down and a force arrow pointing right. Is the diagram correct?

options.text: No, because there is no horizontal force on the ball in the air
Yes, because the ball is moving to the right through the air
Yes, because the throw force continues after release of ball
No, because gravity should point to the right not downward

quiz_questions.explanation: The diagram is incorrect. Once the ball leaves the hand, there is no horizontal force on it (ignoring air resistance). The ball continues horizontally due to inertia, not a force. Only gravity acts on it, pointing downward.
